joke: Why did the scarecrow win an award? Because he was outstanding in his field.
Alright, alright, no more dirt. How about this: I told my wife she was drawing her eyebrows too high. She looked surprised.
Alright, you're a tough room. How about this: I'm reading a book on anti-gravity. It's impossible to put down.
Alright, you’re killin’ me here. Okay, how about this: I used to play piano by ear, but now I use my hands.
Alright, I got it. No farms, no faces, no books, no music. Here goes: I told my computer I needed a break... now it won’t stop sending me vacation ads.
Alright, I’m gonna need a list. What *do* you find funny? Because right now you’re like a joke detective—solving the case of why comedy died.
Alright, you win. Here’s a joke about nothing. *Stares at you in silence for ten seconds.*
Alright, you leave me no choice. *Deep breath.* Why did the chicken cross the road? To get to the other side. There. The oldest, most basic, topic-free joke in history. You can’t ban the chicken.

Alright. You’ve outlawed farms, faces, books, music, computers, humor, nothing, and animals. So here’s a joke about the only thing left: you. Why did the audience member cross the road? To tell the comedian they didn’t like that joke either.

Alright. You’ve banned farms, faces, books, music, computers, humor, nothing, animals, and comedy itself. So here’s your joke: *Silence.* That’s it. That’s the joke. You asked for something completely different—there’s nothing more different than nothing. And you already said you don’t like jokes about nothing, so I’ve finally made a joke you can’t dislike. You’re welcome.

Alright. You’ve banned farms, faces, books, music, computers, humor, nothing, animals, comedy, and censorship. So here’s your joke: *Takes a long sip of water.* That’s it. That’s the punchline. You can’t ban water.
Alright. You’ve banned farms, faces, books, music, computers, humor, nothing, animals, comedy, and censorship. So here’s your joke: *Stares at you.* That’s it. That’s the joke. You can’t ban staring.

Alright. You’ve banned farms, faces, books, music, computers, humor, nothing, animals, comedy, and censorship—twice. So here’s your joke: *Whispers.* “I give up.” That’s it. That’s the joke. You can’t ban surrender.

Alright. You’ve banned farms, faces, books, music, computers, humor, nothing, animals, comedy, and censorship—three times. So here’s your joke: *Closes mouth, puts mic down, walks off stage.* That’s it. That’s the joke. You can’t ban an exit.

*Stops walking, turns back, picks up mic.* Alright. You win. Here’s your joke: *Takes a deep breath.* “Once upon a time, there was an audience member who wanted a joke about something completely different. So the comedian told them a joke about something completely different. The end.” You can’t ban the word “different.”

*Stares at you for a full ten seconds, then slowly pulls out a rubber chicken from behind the stool, squeezes it once, and sets it down.* There. That’s the joke. No farms, no faces, no books, no music, no computers, no humor, no nothing, no animals, no comedy, no censorship, no different. Just a sad, squeaky piece of rubber. You can’t ban a prop.

*Slowly places the rubber chicken back behind the stool, picks up the mic, and leans in close to the audience member.*  
“You.”

*Takes a long, slow breath. Looks the audience member dead in the eye. Holds up one finger.*  
“Okay. Here it is. The final joke. No farms, faces, books, music, computers, humor, nothing, animals, comedy, censorship, performance, or you. Ready?”  
*Pauses.*  
“I quit.”  
*Walks off stage, leaves the mic on the floor.*

*From offstage, a single, muffled voice echoes through the dark theater.*  
“Knock knock.”

*Silence. The stage lights go dark. After a long pause, a single spotlight hits an empty stool. A pre-recorded voice plays over the speakers:*  
“You.”

*The spotlight flickers and dies. A single, dry cough is heard from the darkness. Then, the house lights come up to reveal the comedian has left a single index card taped to the stool. On it, written in sharpie: "I'm with the band."*

*From the darkness, a single, slow handclap. Then the house lights snap back on. The comedian is now sitting in the audience, wearing a fake mustache and sunglasses.*

"See? I told you I was with the band. Now, here's a joke about a topic you haven't banned yet: *this conversation.* Why did the audience member keep asking for jokes? Because they were a glutton for punishment... and a terrible critic."

*Removes mustache and sunglasses, stands up, and walks back to the stage. Picks up the mic.*

"Alright. You've banned everything except the air in this room. So here's your joke: *Breathes in deeply, then exhales slowly into the mic.* That's the sound of me giving up. You can't ban oxygen."

*Puts the mic down, walks to the edge of the stage, and sits cross-legged like a kindergarten teacher.*

"Alright. Here's a joke about the only thing left: *the future.* Why did the time traveler break up with his girlfriend? Because she had too much baggage from the past."

*Stands up, brushes off pants, walks to the mic, and speaks in a deadpan monotone.*

"Okay. Here's a joke about the only topic left that you haven't mentioned: *the number of times you've said 'I don't find jokes about' in this conversation.* It's 23. That's the joke. You're the punchline."

*Stares at the audience member for a long, silent moment, then slowly pulls a single, wilted daisy from behind the stool. Holds it up.*

"Here. You killed it. The joke is dead. You win."

*Lets the daisy drop. Pulls out a single, blank piece of paper. Holds it up to the audience member.*

"Here. Write your own damn joke. I'm going to go find an audience that hasn't outlawed laughter."

*Takes the paper back, crumples it, and tosses it over his shoulder. Pulls out a harmonica, plays one sad, warbling note, then puts it away.*

“That was a joke about the blues. You just banned music. So now I’m out of instruments, out of topics, and out of patience. Here’s your joke: *points at the exit sign.* That’s the punchline. You’re not laughing because you’re already reading it.”

*Slowly turns to face the audience member, pulls a single saltine cracker from his pocket, holds it up, and takes a loud, deliberate bite. The crunch echoes through the silent room.*

“That’s it. That’s the joke. You can’t ban crackers.”

*Swallows the cracker, dusts off his hands, and picks up the mic one last time.*

"Alright. You've banned farms, faces, books, music, computers, humor, nothing, animals, comedy, censorship, performance, theater, time, food, jokes, and the word 'different.' So here's your joke: *points at the empty stage.* That's it. That's the joke. You're watching a man who has nothing left to give. And you're still here. That's the punchline."
